Because there's a lot of markets like I have interest in RC planes, I have interest in CNC machining and stuff like that. And I have a rabid interest in it, but there's a tiny market for it. So what I want to do is pull up some things here. Okay. So one of the things that I want to say about markets is that markets are not created. Markets are needs, wants, desires, fears that are built into us from our caveman and cavewoman DNA of what's built into us. And we've talked about this in some of the VSLs that you guys have seen that teach some of this stuff and that we really are, everything is selling to emotion. And sometimes we just get a little more sophisticated away from the core emotion that we're feeling or wanting or motivates us into something that's a little, seems a little more abstract. So, but we always have to know, and that's what Clayton Makepeace talks about, we always have to know what our dominant resident emotion is for when people buy stuff like that. A good example of that would be a couple of things. One would be when someone buys insurance. Okay. When someone buys insurance, you think, oh, they're buying that so that they can feel safe if their house burns down or something like that. What they're really doing is buying peace of mind now. They're buying the fact that they feel anxious about that whole vision of something that's farther down the road. So security and stuff like that is we'll get into Maslow in a much deeper way. Okay, the first hundred million was written a long time ago. But it turns out that the same interests and desires they had back then that are on a DNA level are still the same ones we have today. They haven't really changed that much. Okay, so you're going to find a lot of stuff that's, you know, relationship based. It's love based. It's sex based. If you look at the first hundred million, it's a guy that released a whole bunch of little blue books and these little blue books, he sold them for a nickel a piece, but you had to buy 20 of them. And this was kind of a good thing because they maybe they didn't want 20 books. So they started, you know, adding, adding, just adding anything that could and they could make an excuse that all they were buying this title just to get the, because they had to buy 20. So they just picked one. But what they really did was they found out what people are really interested in. And you can talk about everything's connected in sidelines is that book reveals that when they changed the title of those books and basically the headline or the bullet in the ad, they found dramatic differences in the sales that were, that were generated by changing the title and seeing the little nuances and concept changes that were very, that were significant, very significant. So if you want to get the first hundred million, that's fine. You don't have to push on that really hard for the first hundred million. So anything above 20 is probably high and a lot of affiliates are promoting it. So if it's got a lot of gravity, then that would indicate that it's got a lot of gravity. That would indicate that it's got, you know, there's some anecdotal evidence there that it's selling well. Anecdotal, I would think this is all numeric. I think it would almost be scientific proof that it had some, I mean, unless someone's rigging the game. Well, what happens is you get a lot of people who have a promotion, a launch. So the gravity for that launch is going to be very high for a short period. And you're right, it's not anecdotal, it's exactly numeric, but there's different ways that people can gain the system a little bit there. But generally, it's fairly accurate, but you just got to be aware of the fact that if it's just happened, it's going to have a high gravity as opposed to a product. A lot of more experienced affiliates will look for products that have some kind of longevity to them or have trended well or they'll look for, if you look here, you'll see that the initial sale is X and then the average percentage of sales has got some interesting things there. So they might have a product that sells for $100, but their average sale price is lower because it's taken into account refunds and stuff. Okay. So I know people specialize in reoccurring products and then the different niches. And there's other services out there that specialize in deciphering. Clickbank. Clickbank Engine is one off the top of my head where people look at these numbers for people who are looking for industries or niches to get involved with. Okay. And then you probably have a way of figuring out if they've been around a while or if they just, if they're new kid on the block or they've been around for, they've got some longevity, probably maybe by how they list how, when they've been announced their product, like, did they open this product to the market a year ago? Here, I'll give you an example. It's down here. It's interesting because you see here survived the end of the day. It's got this prepper stuff. And then you've got Texture X back, which is Chris Soudat, which we're going to bring on the show also, which we have a bunch of. Clickbank experts looks like we're going to have been coming on board. But the nice thing about this is that you not only do you get to find out what's a hot market, but you also get to see how they're doing it, how they're marketing. How are they selling it? What's the voice that they're using to the market? What's their message? How are they positioning this? Getting some, if we can figure out, because I'm not an expert in this area, but if you can figure out how they've been around and all this stuff, these are real numbers. And so you know it's worth paying attention to how they're doing it. So that's kind of what people do when they see Agora's list, early to rise and stuff like that. Then you start looking at... They're going to actually buy the product itself. So, coming back to the page and actually purchasing the product. And this is my recommendation. While you're purchasing the product itself, you record it using Camtasia or something else. Because you're going to look at their upsells or downsells, all that information. And you're going to basically model that. You're going to mimic that for your campaign. And then you're just going to go to all the places that they advertise. Because you already find that you already have that information on. And you're going to advertise on those particular networks or that website. The key thing I would do when setting this up is when you're going to advertise on those websites. Try it out for us. Start small. And if it converts, start scaling it up. But go directly to the advertiser himself. Because you better get a way better deal. Sometimes they'll even give you a breakdown of the demographics. And they'll tell you what time of day to run your ads, what type of clicks, who else is running on their network. If you find the competition and you give them that information, they'll give you the data. They'll let you know what's performing really well and help build out your campaign. It's relevant because in my own practice, if I get different people from different industries, who want to talk to me, what I try and do is they fill in a questionnaire and then I build what I call a flash sheet, which is basically an information sheet, which is as much detail about that industry as possible or their website or to really understand what you're going to write about or what you're going to be given advice about. Some of the services, what runs where, for example, if it's a very small niche, it won't have it. It doesn't have any information on it. It has to be of a certain size. But basically, a lot of people, they think, I'm going to start a business or they're going to do something, and they gather all the information and off they go and try and make a business, which is wrong, I think.
